comments.text: To see why $f(n) < \sqrt{n} + 1$ holds, let $\delta$ be the minimum degree of a graph with $n$ vertices that does not contain a $C_4$ and define, for every vertex $v$, the set $S_v$ of all unordered pairs of distinct neighbours of $v$. Now, if $S_v \cap S_w \neq \emptyset$ for some distinct vertices $v$ and $w$, say $(x,y) \in S_v \cap S_w$, then $v, x, w, y$ would be a $C_4$. In order words, to prevent the graph from containing a $C_4$, we need to have that all sets $S_v$ are disjoint. Now we note that, in total, there are $\frac{1}{2}n(n-1)$ unordered pairs of vertices, while $S_v$ contains at least $\frac{1}{2} \delta (\delta-1)$ pairs for every vertex $v$. We therefore get, assuming the sets $S_v$ are pairwise disjoint, \begin{align*} \frac{1}{2}n(n-1) &\ge \sum_v |S_v| \\ &\ge \sum_v \frac{1}{2}\delta(\delta-1) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}n\delta(\delta-1). \end{align*}In other words, $n-1 \ge \delta (\delta - 1)$, so that in particular $\delta < \sqrt{n} + \frac{1}{2}$. Note that this proof would have also worked for the average instead of minimum degree. As for the lower bound $f(n) > \big(1 - o(1)\big)\sqrt{n}$, the polarity graph induced by the projective plane (see Theorem $1$ in this $1966$ paper by Erdős, Rényi and Sós for a construction) has $n = q^2 + q + 1$ vertices and minimum degree $q = \big(1 - o(1)\big)\sqrt{n}$, while it does not contain a $C_4$.
